coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated Co-Zn ferrite: Structural, morphological, dielectric, and magnetic properties
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
coredata.dc:creator: Rashid, Sania
Perveen, Saima
Hafeez, Saiqa
uddin Asad, Samsaam
Khan, Muhammad Zarrar
Azad, Fahad
coredata.dc:description: GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite was synthesized using a facile sonication-assisted approach. X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), elemental dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), impedance analyzer and vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) were employed to characterize the synthesized samples. The XRD data confirmed the formation of GQDs, Co-Zn ferrite and GQDs/Co-Zn ferrite nanocomposite with no detectable impurity peaks. FTIR results identified the presence of GQDs in GQDs/Co-Zn ferrite nanocomposite. The morphological study revealed the decoration of spherical GQDs on the surface of Co-Zn ferrite. Complex impedance plane plots showed the contribution of the relaxation phenomenon associated with grain and grain boundaries in observed dielectric properties of the nanocomposite. The inclusion of GQDs led to the enhancement of the dielectric constant of the nanocomposite at low frequencies. GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 showed superparamagnetic properties which makes it a potential material to be used as a contrast agent in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4
Dielectric properties
Magnetic properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of GQDs, Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.
WH Plot of (a) Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and (b) GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.

Fluorescence spectra of GQDs under different excitation wavelegths indicating blue fluorescence. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) FTIR Spectra of GQDs, Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and (b) enlarged-view of the fingerprint region of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 while inset shows the same of GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.
SEM micrographs of (a) Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and (b) GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, the inset in each figure shows enlarged view of the encirlced region.
Particle size distribution of (a) Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and (b) GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.
EDX Spectrum of (a) Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and (b) GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.
Nyquist plots of (a) Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and (b) GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.
Bode plot of (a) Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and (b) GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.
Dielectric constant (ε′) vs log f for Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.
σAC vs log f (Hz) Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, Jonscher’s power Fitted data for Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.
Magnetization vs field H curves of GQDs, Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 (Inset Enlarged-view to show coercivity of GQDs, Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 & GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4).
Structural parameters of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite.
Electrical impedance parameters of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.
Comparison of different previous works on composite with giant permittivity.
Dielectric parameters (ε′, σ, n, A) of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated Co-Zn ferrite: Structural, morphological, dielectric, and magnetic properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Natural Sciences (SNS), National University of Sciences and Technology (NUST), Islamabad, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National University of Sciences and Technology (NUST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Natural Sciences (SNS), National University of Sciences and Technology (NUST), Islamabad, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electronic and Magnetic Materials Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering (SCME), National University of Sciences and Technology (NUST), Islamabad, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering (SCME)
National University of Sciences and Technology (NUST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electronic and Magnetic Materials Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering (SCME), National University of Sciences and Technology (NUST), Islamabad, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite was synthesized using a facile sonication-assisted approach. X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), elemental dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), impedance analyzer and vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) were employed to characterize the synthesized samples. The XRD data confirmed the formation of GQDs, Co-Zn ferrite and GQDs/Co-Zn ferrite nanocomposite with no detectable impurity peaks. FTIR results identified the presence of GQDs in GQDs/Co-Zn ferrite nanocomposite. The morphological study revealed the decoration of spherical GQDs on the surface of Co-Zn ferrite. Complex impedance plane plots showed the contribution of the relaxation phenomenon associated with grain and grain boundaries in observed dielectric properties of the nanocomposite. The inclusion of GQDs led to the enhancement of the dielectric constant of the nanocomposite at low frequencies. GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 showed superparamagnetic properties which makes it a potential material to be used as a contrast agent in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dielectric materials with high colossal permittivity are potential materials for the electronic industry due to their use in energy storage devices, actuators, sonars, and micromotors [1,2]. Ferrites due to their low conductivity, high electrical resistivity, and strong chemical stability are promising candidates [3,4]. Furthermore, the unique magnetic properties of the ferrites [5] also make them a suitable material for magnetic and electric tunable devices [6], magnetic data storage [7] and biomedical applications [8]. Cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) has an inverse spinel structure with two sub-lattice A and B; Fe3+ ions reside on the A and B sites while Co2 + ions occupy B sites. The ions' interaction at octahedral sites has been found responsible for the dielectric and magnetic response of the cobalt ferrites [6,9].

Zn doping in Co-ferrite has been reported to enhance the dielectric properties of CoFe2O4 and reduce dielectric losses. Atif et al. reported that the dielectric and magnetic properties of cobalt ferrite are strongly affected by Zn doping concentration and an optimized concentration is required to achieve the best results [10]. Tatarchuk et al. studied the ferromagnetic behaviour of Zn-doped CoFe2O4 and disclosed that saturation magnetization (Ms) decreases with the increase in Zn concentration. Among Zn2+ substituted CoFe2O4 nanoparticles (Co1-xZnxFe2O4), x = 0.5 has the maximum symmetry than any other x value. This symmetric structure results in better crystallinity, thermal stability and improved dielectric and magnetic properties [11]. This motivated us to choose Co-Zn ferrite with the composition Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 because of its unique magnetic and dielectric properties, making it an effective material for electromagnetic interference shielding and power handling capabilities of antennas.

Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been found useful for the enhancement of dielectric and magnetic properties of ferrites. T. Chen et al. [12] synthesized GQDs/cobalt ferrite/polyvinylidene fluoride (CFO-PVDF) nanocomposite and reported an exponential increase in the dielectric constant from a value of 60 to 95,000 and a decrease in dielectric losses when GQDs were added in CFO-PVDF. Similarly, Z. Naghshbandi et al. observed that the magnetic and catalytic activity were enhanced by using GQDs nanocomposite with Ni and Co ferrite [13]. Therefore, this study is aimed to disclose the effect of GQDs on the dielectric and magnetic properties of the Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 host. The addition of conducting GQDs to relatively less conductive Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 creates interfaces between GQDs and Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 which gives rise to additional capacitance leading to enhancement in dielectric properties of the nanocomposite.

In this work, we prepared GQDs, Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, and their nanocomposite using facile synthesis routes. X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscope (SEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), impedance spectroscopy and vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) were employed to characterize these samples. GQDs inclusion in Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 was found to enhance the dielectric and magnetic properties of the synthesized nanocomposite.

Citric acid (C6H8O7), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn (NO3)2·6H2O), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co (NO3)2·6H2O), iron nitrate nano hydrate (Fe (NO3)2·9H2O) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich and used without further purification.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by direct pyrolyzing citric acid as reported by Y. Dong et al. [14]. 2 gm of citric acid monohydrate was added into a beaker and heated at 200 0C for 35 min. During the first five minutes, solid citric acid turned into a colourless liquid, which was further heated for 30 min until its colour changed from yellow to orange. The resulting product was poured slowly into 100 ml of 0.176 M aq. NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. The pH of the final solution was found to be 7. The GQDs were then purified by using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500 Da) for 48 h in deionized water.

Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 was synthesized using the coprecipitation method. 0.5 M aq. solution of cobalt nitrate hexahydrate and zinc nitrate hexahydrate each was added into 2 M aq. solution of iron nitrate nano hydrate under constant stirring. After 30 min of stirring, 3 M aq. NaOH solution was added to the above solution dropwise. The resulting solution was kept on stirring for another 30 min. The obtained solution was then heated at 70 °C temperature for 1 hr. The precipitates were then collected using centrifugation and washed several times with deionized water until pH 7 was achieved. The sample was then dried at 100 °C and calcined at 600 °C for 3 hrs.

The nanocomposite was synthesized using ultrasonication-assisted mixing of GQDs and Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4. 5 ml of GQDs solution and 0.8 g of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 dissolved into 20 ml of deionized water were sonicated separately for 2 hrs. The solutions were then mixed and further sonicated for 2 hrs. The solution was then dried at 70 °C into an oven.

X-ray diffractometer (DRON-8, Bourevestnik) having Cu-kα radiation of wavelength 0.15406 nm with spectral resolution of 0.497 Å was employed for structural characterization. FTIR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Spectrum) was used to study the presence of GQDs in the prepared nanocomposite. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM JEOL JSM-6390) was used to study morphological properties. Room temperature impedance analysis (Wayne Kerr LCR 6500B) was performed to analyze dielectric properties. The samples were pelletized using a hydraulic press under a pressure of 5 kN for 5 min. A vibrating sample magnetometer (DXV-220, Dexing) was employed to disclose magnetic properties.

X-ray diffraction patterns of the synthesized GQDs, Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 1 . No suspicious peak was observed in any samples that exclude the possibility of impurity phase. XRD pattern of GQDs shows a single broad peak at 2θ = 21.7°, which could be indexed as (002) of hexagonal structure [15,16]. The broadness of the diffraction peak indicates disarranged structure (amorphous nature) due to the nanoscale size of the GQDs [16]. XRD pattern of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 exhibits characteristic diffraction peaks: (200), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511) and (440) of cubic structure with space group Fd3m. These peaks are in accordance with JCPDS card no. 22-1086 and 22-1012 of CoFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4, respectively. The XRD pattern of GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite presents (311), (400), (511) and (440) diffraction peaks of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 with relatively lower intensity and higher background noise. Moreover, the characteristic peak of GQDs is absent in the XRD pattern of the nanocomposite. The reduction in diffraction peaks intensity and higher background noise might be related to the presence of less-crystalline GQDs in the nanocomposite. The absence of GQDs characteristic peak could be due to its lower content, less intensity of (002) peak [17–19] and the presence of GQDs on the surface of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 particles. The d-spacing, lattice parameter (a) and cell volume of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite was calculated from (311) diffraction peak. The data is shown in Table 1 . The lattice constant value of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 is found to be higher as compared to pure cobalt ferrite because of the larger radius of Zn2+ (0.6Ao) than Co2+ (0.58Ao) [11]. A reduction in the lattice parameter, d-spacing, and unit cell volume was also observed upon nanocomposite formation. Williamson-Hall strain analysis was performed to further analyze the crystalline quality of the samples.

Williamson-Hall (WH) method was used to calculate the average crystallite size (D) and microstrain ( ∊ ) of the synthesized samples using the following equation [19]
(1)
β cos θ =
k λ
D
+ 4 ∊ sin θ

Where β is full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the peak with Bragg’s angleθ, k is the dimensionless constant, λ is the wavelength of the X-ray beam (1.5406 Å). WH plots of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 are shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), respectively.

The calculated values of average crystallite size and microstrain of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 are given in Table 1. The nanocomposite showed higher average crystallite size along with lower microstrain value as compared to pure Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, which indicates improvement of crystalline quality due to the inclusion of GQDs. This result suggests that GQDs provide robust support to Co-Zn ferrite crystallites and lead to enhancement in the crystalline quality [17]. Therefore, the reduction in lattice parameter, d-spacing, and unit cell volume can be explained by a decrease in the microstrain value of the GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite.

Fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy was employed to study the fluorescence properties of synthesized GQDs. FL spectrum of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths is shown in Fig. 3 . The emission spectra of GQDs lie in the blue region. This confirmed that the prepared GQDs are blue in colour and have uniform Sp2 structure [14]. Furthermore, the GQDs solution exhibited blue colour when placed under the UV lamp which also confirmed the formation of blue-coloured GQDs.

FTIR spectra of all samples: GQDs, Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 are shown in Fig. 4 (a). The enlarged fingerprint region of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 spectra is presented in Fig. 4(b). The spectrum of GQDs showed absorption bands at 669.23 cm−1, 1384.88 cm−1, 1637 cm−1, 2076.17 cm−1 and 3449.38 cm−1. The absorption band of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 were found at 450.7 cm−1 and 592.31 cm−1, whereas GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 showed peaks in the fingerprint region at 398.14 cm−1 and 585.34 cm−1 along with functional groups at 1384.88 cm−1, 1637 cm−1, and 3449.38 cm−1. The absorption peaks at 669.23 cm−1, 1384.88 cm−1, 1637 cm−1, 2076.17 cm−1 and 3449.38 cm−1 are attributed to COC, COH, CC, CC, and OH vibrations, respectively, due to presence of GQDs. A stronger absorption band at 400.53 cm−1 (393.04 cm−1) and weaker at 597.31 cm−1 (591.24 cm−1) in Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 (GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4) correspond to vibrations of octahedral and tetrahedral metal ion complexes, respectively. The weaker transmittance % of these vibrations might be due to the stronger bonding of the tetrahedral and octahedral ions with oxygen and magnetic hindrance.

The bond length of metal–oxygen (FeO) at the tetrahedral (A) and octahedral (B) sites is 1.89 Å and 1.99 Å , respectively. The smaller bond length at the A site is due to the existence of stronger covalent bonding at this site, resulting in weak absorption peaks. However, weak bonding at the B site led to large bond length and stronger absorption bands as can be seen from the enlarged view of the spectra (Fig. 4b). The metal–oxygen absorption bands of GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 shifted to lower wavenumber due to formation of nanocomposite with GQDs [5,8,20]. This shifting of absorption bands in nanocomposite might be due to the formation of a heterogeneous interface between GQDs and Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: SEM and EDX spectroscopy were employed to investigate the morphology and the elemental composition of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 respectively. Fig. 5 (a–b) presents SEM micrographs of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4. Large particles can be seen in the SEM micrographs of both samples; however, relatively larger particles were observed in Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4. Inset in Fig. 5(a–b) depicts higher-resolution SEM micrographs of the encircled area shown in the corresponding micrograph. The presence of bigger size particles in both samples might be due to the permanent magnetic moment of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 [11]. Inset in Fig. 5(b) shows spherical-shaped GQDs decorating the Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 particles which is consistent with the literature [13]. Furthermore, the dispersion of GQDs in the nanohybrid samples has reduced grain size to some extent (as seen in the micrographs) which led to smaller particle (grain) size and enhanced surface area (grain boundaries) [22].

Fig. 6 (a–b) shows particle size distribution of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4. The mean particle size was estimated to be ∼77.7 nm for Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and ∼23.57 nm for GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 respectively. Fig. 7 (a–b) depicts EDX spectra of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4. The existence of cobalt, zinc, iron, and oxygen was detected in both samples. The carbon content in both samples has contribution from carbon tape used for holding samples and removing charging effects. However, the GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite has a higher carbon concentration due to the inclusion of GQDs in the nanocomposite. Furthermore, the content of oxygen in nanohybrid was found higher than pure ferrites due to the existence of oxygenate functionalities in GQDs.

Impedance spectroscopy is a powerful technique to study dielectric properties along with resolving the contribution of extrinsic and intrinsic factors responsible for these properties. In polycrystalline materials, the response to an applied AC signal arises from extrinsic (material-electrode interface) and intrinsic elements (grains, and grain boundaries). The response of extrinsic elements dominates at higher frequencies whereas intrinsic elements show their response in intermediate (grains) and low (grain boundaries) frequency ranges. Nyquist plot (Z′ vs Z″) of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 is shown in Fig. 8 (a–b); the frequency of AC signal increases from right-to-left in this graph. The Nyquist plot usually contains one or more than one semi-circular arc in different frequency regions. The diameter of each semi-circular arc on the
Z ′
axis indicates the charge transfer resistance of the corresponding microstructural component, and the sum of these diameters gives total resistance. The intersection of a semi-circular arc at the high-frequency side (extreme left) indicates the presence of electrode resistance. In both samples, the intersection of the semi-circular arc with
Z ′
axis at the high-frequency side is approximately at the origin which shows the presence of very small electrode resistance. Two semi-circular arcs, a complete one at a higher frequency side (left) and an incomplete one with a larger diameter at a lower frequency side (right), were observed in the Nyquist plot of both Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4. The presence of two semi-circular arcs shows the existence of two relaxation phenomena with distinct relaxation times in both samples. In oxide semiconductors, grain boundaries appear to be more resistive to charge transport as compared to grains because of the trapping nature of oxygen-deficient defects and dangling bonds present at grain boundaries. Therefore, grain boundaries owing to their higher resistivity act as thin dielectric layers between relatively conducting grains. Furthermore, grain boundaries also have higher capacitance due to the inverse relationship of capacitance with the thickness of the dielectric layer. Hence, the complete semi-circular arc at higher frequencies and an incomplete larger one at the lower frequency can be assigned to grains and grain boundaries, respectively. The diameter of the semi-circular arc at the higher frequency side of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 is greater as compared to GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 indicating higher conductivity due to the conductive nature of the graphene quantum dots [23,24].

The data was fitted for equivalent electrical circuit model Rs(CgRgb)(CgbRgbCPE) for both samples and shown in Fig. 8 (solid line). Rs represents the resistance of the interface between sample and electrode; Cg, Cgb, Rg and Rgb denote capacitance and resistance of the grain (g) and grain boundary (gb), respectively, and CPE shows constant phase element. CPE is used in the fitting of Nyquist plots due to the deviation of capacitance from its ideal behaviour because of the involvement of more than one relaxation phenomenon with close relaxation times. The capacitance of CPE can be calculated using the following equation: C=
R
( 1 - n )
n
Q
1 n
, where C, R and Q represent capacitance, resistance, and constant phase element, respectively. The value of n varies from 0 to 1 indicating pure resistive to pure capacitive behaviour of CPE [25,26]. The fitting parameters of both the samples are given in Table 2 .

The frequency dependence of the imaginary part of impedance (Z″) and electrical modulus (M″) is useful in studying relaxation processes and charge transport phenomena of a material. Fig. 9 (a–b) display the Bode plot of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4. Z″-Bode plot of both samples shows two relaxation peaks: an incomplete peak at low frequency and a complete peak at the higher frequency. The dominant incomplete peak at the lower frequency side corresponds to the relaxation process associated with grain boundaries whereas the weaker complete peak at the higher frequency side is related to grain relaxation. The dominance of Z″ peak associated with grain boundaries relaxation, in both samples shows higher resistive nature of grain boundaries as compared to grains. Furthermore, each relaxation process has an associated relaxation time
( τ
r
)
which is inversely proportional to relaxation peak frequency and can be calculated by the following expression:
f r
=
1
2 π
τ r
. The relaxation peak corresponding to grains appeared at relatively lower frequency for Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 (Fig. 9(a)) as compared to GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 (Fig. 9(b)). This shifting of relaxation peak indicates a reduction of relaxation time due to the inclusion of GQDs in Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.

The modulus formalism (M″ vs log(f)) helps differentiate the involvement of short-range and long-range carriers in the conduction mechanism. The occurrence of M″ peak at the high and low-frequency side indicates conduction due to short and long-range carriers, respectively. Moreover, the dominant peak in this plot indicates the most conductive element [27]. In the nanocomposite, the peak moved toward higher frequency suggesting an increase in the frequency range over which carriers are mobile between grains [28]. The peak match of Z″ and M″ identifies the Debye type behaviour of charge carriers, and on the contrary, the mismatch of peaks indicates non-Debye type behaviour [27,29].

The modulus formalism (M″ vs log f) is used to analyze the conduction mechanism due to the short-range and long-range mobility of carriers. The M″ vs log f of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 are shown in Fig. 9. Both curves show a broad and a partial peak which indicates the occurrence of two conduction phenomena in grains. The broad M″ peak of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 lies in the frequency range of 105–106, while it shifts to a higher frequency range (106–107) for GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4. The broad peak at a relatively lower frequency is attributed to long-range hopping, while the high-frequency peak shows the short-range hopping of carriers. At a frequency range below M″ peak, the charge carriers exhibit long-range mobility across grains, whereas at higher frequencies these are limited to their potential well and mobile only within the grains [30]. The shifting of peak to a higher frequency range in GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 shows that charge carriers in grains are mobile in a very short range compared to Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4.

Frequency dependence of the dielectric constant was analyzed to further understand dielectric properties, the data is shown in Fig. 10 . A decreasing trend of dielectric constant with frequency was observed, which can be explained by Maxwell-Wagner interfacial polarization and Koop’s theory [31]. The colossal permittivity (∼
10
3
)
was observed in Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 sample at low frequencies. In cobalt zinc ferrite, the polarization occurs due to the hole (Co2+- Co3+) and electron (Fe2+-Fe3+) exchange under the applied field. The higher permittivity at low frequency can be explained by electron accumulation at the grain boundaries because of their resistive nature, which gives rise to high interfacial polarization. The hole and electron exchange ceases at high frequency, reducing electron accumulation at grain boundaries and polarization [32]. However, a significant increase in the dielectric constant of GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 as compared to Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 was observed at low frequency. This rise in the dielectric constant can be explained by the addition of conductive GQDs in the Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 matrix. GQDs isolate the Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 matrix [4] and create an interface between GQDs and Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 [24]. These interfaces between conductive GQDs and relatively less conducting Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 behave as parallel plate capacitors [32], and increase the dielectric constant of the nanocomposite. The comparison of current work with others is given in Table 3 . In this work, we realized high permittivity in GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 along with good magnetic properties (given in the next section) making it to be an excellent material for enhancing the power handling capabilities of antennas, electromagnetic interference shielding and power electronics.

Fig. 11 illustrates the AC conductivity of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite as a function of angular frequency (ω) in logarithmic scale. Both samples showed a similar response in the whole frequency domain. At low and medium values of log(ω), the AC conductivity remained independent of the frequency, however, a strong dependence was observed at higher log(ω). Moreover, the AC conductivity of GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite remained higher than Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 in the whole frequency range due to the inclusion of conductive GQDs. The dispersion response of AC conductivity at high angular frequency is explained by Jonscher power law [26]:
(2)
σ
Ac
=
σ
dc
+ A
ω
s

Where
σ
Ac
and
σ
dc
represent frequency dependent and independent conductivity, respectively; A is polarizability constant; ω is the angular frequency (2πf), and s (0 < s less than 1) is the Jonscher’s power law factor which identifies the type of interaction between charge carriers. The Jonscher power law fitted curves are also shown in Fig. 11 with solid lines. The fitting parameters are given in Table 4 . The value of s in both samples is less than 1 indicating the contribution of long- and short-range carriers to the conductivity. Moreover, the value of s for GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite is smaller than Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 specifying enhanced conductivity as well as polarization due to increased interaction between charge carriers.
σ
dc
value calculated from the fitted data shows a close approximation with experimentally measured values given in Table 3. In Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, at a low angular frequency range, σ
dc showed an independent response due to the long-range mobility of carriers towards the grain boundary. However, at high angular frequency, the short-range hole (Co2+ Co3+) and electron (Fe2+ Fe3+) exchange are dominant which contributes to the conductivity [38]. These localized states act as potential wells and carriers move from one well to another through a tunnelling mechanism. In GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 case, a percolative network was formed between GQDs and Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4. The conductivity was low at lower angular frequencies because the fillers were blocked by the ceramic layer, however, at higher angular frequency range the filler and the matrix particles both contribute to the conductivity due to diminishment of the blocking effect of filler particles and hence AC conductivity increases [39].

A vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) was employed to determine the magnetic properties of Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 at room temperature with an applied magnetic field of 10 kOe. The magnetic properties of spinel ferrites depend on particle size, doping, porosity, and site preference of cations in spinel lattice. Fig. 12 . represents magnetic hysteresis loops of GQDs, Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4, and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 samples. All the samples show negligible coercivity (Hc) which is indicative of the existence of single-domain particles. The saturation magnetization (Ms) of GQDs, Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 was found to be 5.9 emu/g, 29 emu/g and 31 emu/g which is comparable to other reports [40,41]. Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 showed coercivity value ∼65 Oe which is also in agreement with literature [42,43]. The cations occupy tetrahedral (A) and octahedral (B) sites in the spinel structure of ferrites. Zn2+ ions preferably reside on the A site, Co2+ ions on the B site while Fe3+ ions occupy, both, the A and B sites. The magnetic moment of Zn2+, Co2+ and Fe3+ are zero, 3µB and 5µB, respectively. Hence, the inclusion of high content of non-magnetic Zn2+ ions to CoFe2O4 leads to enormous Zn2+ occupancies at the A site. This causes a large percentage of Fe3+ ions to reside at the B site and reduces magnetic ions at the A site. As a result, it weakens A-B superexchange interactions and favours canted spin arrangements on the B site, reducing the net magnetization of zinc-substituted cobalt ferrite compared to pure cobalt ferrite [9]. A slight increase in saturation magnetization after the addition of GQDs to Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 might be due to enhanced net spin orientation. However, it could not increase significantly due to the non-magnetic nature of GQDs. The inclusion of GQDs to Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 can add a great feature to the properties of GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 composite due to the fluorescence properties of the quantum dots. Since fluorescence is an essential feature for MRI applications, therefore, GQD/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 seems to be a remarkable candidate for such applications.

Pure GQDs, Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4
, and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite were synthesized using a facile synthesis approach. XRD patterns confirmed the formation of GQDs, Co-Zn ferrite, and nanocomposite. FTIR spectrum confirmed the presence of GQDs in the nanocomposite. EDX confirmed the presence of cobalt, zinc, iron, carbon, and oxygen in these samples. The dielectric properties study revealed ∼10 times enhancement of dielectric constant in GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite at low frequencies. The addition of GQDs resulted in less resistive response and enhanced conductivity. Impedance spectroscopy showed the contribution of both grain and inter-grain boundaries while the Bode plots confirmed the non-Debye type behaviour of samples. The VSM analysis revealed superparamagnetic nature of both Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 and GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 samples. The maximum saturation magnetization (Ms) value was observed in GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 (31 emu/g) having negligible coercivity (Hc) and remanent magnetization (Mr). These results show that GQDs/Co0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 is a potential material for energy storage devices as a capacitive medium and in MRI applications as a contrast agent.

Sania Rashid: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Saima Perveen: Writing – review & editing. Saiqa Hafeez: Writing – original draft. Samsaam uddin Asad: Formal analysis. Muhammad Zarrar Khan: Data curation. Fahad Azad: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
